words: This conversation with GERALD INGLE took place at the Latah County Courthouse
on October 7, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.
GERALD INGLE: During the Depression, it made it pretty rough.
SAM SCHRAGER: Really? Really it was?

speaker: GI
words: My dad was president of the bank, when the bank closed.

speaker: SS
words: You grew up on a farm, right? On Big Bear. Your parents had been

speaker: SS
words: here considerably early, hadn't they?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, the place that I grew up on was my grandfather's old homestead.

speaker: GI
words: Then when my father took over the farm my grandfather built another

speaker: GI
words: house on another piece of land, so we still have that place. In

speaker: GI
words: fact, I own that place now, the old homestead.

speaker: SS
words: What do you remember the farming being like when you were still just

speaker: SS
words: a kid? Was it still early pioneering pretty much or had it passed that

speaker: SS
words: stage.

speaker: GI
words: we a lot of land there after I was a kid and helping, and of course

speaker: GI
words: in those days we used horses. I think in 1923 or '24 was when we

speaker: GI
words: bout our first tractor, farmed with horses up till then.

speaker: SS
words: Were the kids expected to do a lot of that work, a lot of the breaking?

speaker: GI
words: Well, we helped to pile brush and cut brush and stuff like that. We

speaker: GI
words: did some of that after Iwas big enough

speaker: SS
words: If you tried to characterize the condition of the average farmer when

speaker: SS
words: you were still pretty young, you were born in '10, so figure out between the teens

speaker: SS
words: and in the '20's. How were they doing?

speaker: GI
words: They were pretty hard up, really. the country had a lot more people in

speaker: GI
words: it, a lot of people justA160 acres of land, some had a little more

speaker: GI
words: and some had smaller places. They milked cows and sold cream and sold

speaker: GI
words: eggs and sold butter those days. They didn't have creameries so much

speaker: GI
words: then they churned the cream into butter and sold butter. And that was

speaker: GI
words: how they paid their store bills for the food bill

speaker: SS
words: Was that their main source of money?

speaker: GI
words: Their main source was their crop, but as far as the household needs

speaker: GI
words: in the kitchen and put the food on the table a lot of that came from

speaker: GI
words: the- the things they bought in the grocery store- the things they

speaker: GI
words: bought in the grocery store- the sale of butter and eggs and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: They traded at Kendrick?

speaker: GI
words: Right. In that area, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would they go mostly to Long's store, or were there other stores?

speaker: GI
words: There was Long's store and there was Stanton's store and there was

speaker: GI
words: store. There was the three stores when I was a kid. The

speaker: GI
words: Long's store was there where Erickson's is and Stanton's store was

speaker: GI
words: there where the drugstore is and store was where

speaker: GI
words: hardware is.

speaker: SS
words: How often would your family make it into Kendrick?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, I think probably once a week or so, probably.

speaker: SS
words: How far is it?

speaker: GI
words: Nine miles from town. See, my dad bought his first car in 1914, so

speaker: GI
words: I was only four years old when they bought the car. It used to be

speaker: GI
words: back years ago when I remember? most of the farmers went to town on

speaker: GI
words: Saturday afternoons. Took the day off and did their trading,

speaker: GI
words: Would that be just the man of the family or would their wife go?

speaker: GI
words: Some of 'em would take their wi?e? Later on they did more. Probably

speaker: GI
words: earlier there was just the man and maybe take the kids or one of the

speaker: GI
words: kids along with them to town. ^1 used to go to town with my dad

speaker: GI
words: by myself with him, and maybe my brother the next time, took our turns.

speaker: GI
words: It was quite a treat getting to go to

speaker: SS
words: What would you do'.when you would go, in town, as a kid?

speaker: GI
words: I'd just gawk, as I remember now. And I remember one time I went down,

speaker: GI
words: the first time I ever drank soda pop. Got me a bottle of lemon pop,

speaker: GI
words: and I thought that was really something!

speaker: SS
words: Did he do much visiting with other guys?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes. He was on the board of directors of the Cooperative Ware

speaker: GI
words: house, Kendrick Roachdale Company, that's the oldest, one of the

speaker: GI
words: oldest co-operatives in the county.

speaker: SS
words: The Kendrick-?

speaker: GI
words: Roachdale. It's still there. He was on that board of directors for

speaker: GI
words: thirty, forty years, I guess J I don't know, more than that I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a farmer owned co-op?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Farmer owned co-op. The co-ops they have nowdays, they each far

speaker: SS
words: mer had to put up for $100 stock, and of course now the laws are a

speaker: SS
words: little different, I guess. Of course, they could do business with

speaker: SS
words: anybody but these stockholders got to receive dividends from the bus

speaker: SS
words: iness returns

speaker: SS
words: 1 wonder how broad the ownership was, how many people had put in $100.

speaker: GI
words: 0h don't know. I've seen that old stockholders list, there must

speaker: GI
words: have been seventy-five  hundred people. At that time, I'd say

speaker: GI
words: there were a lot more people in the rural area, smaller farms. It

speaker: GI
words: was a pretty good size and he was on that board of directors. He

speaker: GI
words: to go down once a month for the meeting of the board of directors

speaker: GI
words: of the Kendrick Roachdale Company.

speaker: SS
words: Did that come out of the farmers union?

speaker: GI
words: remember they used to have the Farmers' Union, too. Probably origmally

speaker: GI
words: it first started that. I remember Dad used to have a big

speaker: GI
words: Farmers' Union sign on the ' there when I was a kid. I don't know

speaker: GI
words: whether there was a connection with that, but I just imagine it was

speaker: GI
words: sort of an offshot of that co-operative deal.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what your father's attitudes were about bettering the

speaker: SS
words: position of the farmer in a political sense?

speaker: GI
words: I always felt Dad was pretty interested in developing the country and

speaker: GI
words: a real good attitude about developing. He used to get pretty irked

speaker: GI
words: about some of thefpeople that held back on things. He liked to see

speaker: GI
words: good schools and he liked to see good roads. And I remember several

speaker: GI
words: times when he had gone to Boise with different people to try to get

speaker: GI
words: 'em to fix a certain road or to get the state to fix a certain road.

speaker: GI
words: He pretty much liked to see developments made. That's probably what

speaker: GI
words: rubs off on me over here being in public service.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have much idea of how he got- became disposed towards public

speaker: SS
words: service. It seems like a lot of farmers didn't get out beyond their

speaker: SS
words: own place in terms of their civic interests. What gave him that in

speaker: SS
words: clination?

speaker: GI
words: He was working in this grain/co-operative over there and he was also

speaker: GI
words: a director in a bank there for several years, too and he worked with

speaker: GI
words: people all the time. I know he was on the school board, too when I

speaker: GI
words: was in school. And we used to have a local telephone company and he

speaker: GI
words: was always an officer in our local telephone company. That was esta

speaker: GI
words: blished about 1908 or '09, and he was chairman of that a lot and sec

speaker: GI
words: retary a lot. I think he just sort of grew up with it, he sort of

speaker: GI
words: liked to work with people, too. And he got to be county commissioner

speaker: GI
words: at one time, too, in his life. That was in '42 and '43, his was only

speaker: GI
words: one term.

speaker: SS
words: Wonder where King, the nickname King came from.

speaker: GI
words: King came from- that was his name; King David Ingle. His dad's brother

speaker: GI
words: was named King Ingle, too.

speaker: SS
words: That was his first name.

speaker: GI
words: King David Ingle. His uncle's name was the same. I think farther up

speaker: GI
words: the ladder there was somebody named King.

speaker: SS
words: How hard do you feel that he worked?

speaker: GI
words: He worked hard. Dad really worked hard. We were a little bigger

speaker: GI
words: farmers than most of them when I was a kid. We had probably 400,

speaker: GI
words: 350- 400 acres, and we always had hired men when I was a kid and al

speaker: GI
words: ways had a hired girl to help in the house when us kids growing

speaker: GI
words: up.

speaker: SS
words: Year around.

speaker: GI
words: Well, in the summer. Sometimes in the winter we wouldn't

speaker: GI
words: have. We had cattle, some cattle, beef cattle and milk cows, too

speaker: GI
words: and sometimes we'd have a hired man in the winter, but almost always

speaker: GI
words: they'd start generally in the spring, go to say, November or December

speaker: GI
words: sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: Of 300 or 400 acres, how much of that were you farming? Would that

speaker: SS
words: be most of that?

speaker: GI
words: 0h, I could figure about- I suppose it was actually- it was over 300 acres of

speaker: GI
words: farmland; 350 probably.

speaker: SS
words: And did you own all that land, or did you rent?

speaker: GI
words: Well, he rented part of it and some of it he owned and part of it was

speaker: GI
words: my grandfather's land. See, my grandfather never died till 1934- I

speaker: GI
words: think it was '34 or '35- '34, I believe it was, and he lived only half

speaker: GI
words: a mile from us and Grandfather had 320 acres of this land that we far

speaker: GI
words: med. That wasn't all cultivated, but there was two quarters. And

speaker: GI
words: then my dad ownedN80 acres adjoining there and my aunt- and these two

speaker: GI
words: pieces were part of the old estate my dad's sister had an 80 acresand

speaker: GI
words: we farmed those two eighties. That was originally part of my grand

speaker: GI
words: father's acreage.

speaker: SS
words: So it was pretty much all family owned?

speaker: GI
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: Even if he didn't own it himself.

speaker: GI
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: Did he need bigger equipment than most of the farmers at the time?

speaker: GI
words: I don't know whether he had bigger equipment, I guess more people and

speaker: GI
words: more pieces; twobottom gangplow was a big plow in those days in that

speaker: GI
words: area. Two of 'em, see, and take six horses to pull 'em. And lots of

speaker: GI
words: times, with the gangplow and oneLfootburner. walking plow with three

speaker: GI
words: horses on it. I started on one of those when I was ten years old.

speaker: GI
words: I was pretty good size for my age. I remember having the gangplow

speaker: GI
words: ahead and the footburner behind.

speaker: GI
words: Did you work the whole day on the footburner?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes, after I got up to twelve or thirteen years old. I remember

speaker: GI
words: the first time I ever rttn one I was ten years old and I

speaker: GI
words: plowed the garden, and I wanted to run that. I wanted to drive that

speaker: GI
words: team of horses and run that plow so Dad let me plow a few rounds and

speaker: GI
words: then he just went off and let me finish it. I got a little tired be

speaker: GI
words: fore I got done, but after I was up twelve, thirteen years old I ran

speaker: GI
words: it all the time. Then I got so I ran the gangplow. In those days

speaker: GI
words: we bound, and Dad didn't like to run a binder very well, so as soon

speaker: GI
words: as I was big enough, I imagine when I was thirteen, fourteen I started

speaker: GI
words: running a binder for harvest, and my brother shocked. He was a littie

speaker: GI
words: younger. I remember one year there before I startedA . the bin

speaker: GI
words: der; us kids were pretty small and my dad's brother, Dwight Ingle,

speaker: GI
words: he lived with my grandfather, Dad had us three boys shocking. Some

speaker: GI
words: of the shocks- a lot of 'em fell down, but we used to have to shock

speaker: GI
words: the grain, you know. And then a year or two after that I started run

speaker: GI
words: ning the binder and then my brother did the shocking.

speaker: SS
words: 1 have heard that in the early days that with all the work the kids

speaker: SS
words: had to do on a place really got in the way of their going to school.

speaker: SS
words: SS

speaker: GI
words: I mean, they would layout quite abit, in the early days. But by the

speaker: GI
words: time you were growing up did that conflict with your going to school?

speaker: GI
words: No, not in our home. It did with some families, but my dad thought

speaker: GI
words: education^important and we had to milk cows before we went to school

speaker: GI
words: and do chores. We had to feed the hogs. Generally there was four of

speaker: GI
words: us in our family and each one of us had a job to do and that had to

speaker: GI
words: be done before we went to school.

speaker: SS
words: But he didn't believe in having to lay out?

speaker: SS
words: Once in a while, like take in the fall, if we had a wet fall and were

speaker: SS
words: threshing grain and we used to have the company threshing machines

speaker: SS
words: come and thresh, once in a while then if it was a late fall and short

speaker: SS
words: GI

speaker: SS
words: of labor, trying to get things done fast, maybe we'd stay out a day

speaker: SS
words: or two we had the threshers, but as far as my dad was concerned,

speaker: SS
words: he believed in us being in school every day. AMost of the time when

speaker: SS
words: I went to school, I had a perfect attendance record.

speaker: SS
words: Where did you go? Was there a school on the Ridge?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, we had a little local school. It was school. It was

speaker: GI
words: built in 19- 1892, and that was one of the first schools on the Ridge.

speaker: GI
words: There were five schools at that time on the Ridge, but ours was the

speaker: GI
words: central school, it was in the center of the Ridge and it was actually

speaker: GI
words: the biggest school of the five.

speaker: SS
words: How many kids when you were going there?

speaker: GI
words: 1 think the maximum amount at any time was thirty-five. I remember

speaker: GI
words: the year I started to school in the first grade there was twenty-eight

speaker: GI
words: that year and then a year or so after there were thirty-five. I think

speaker: GI
words: it ran between twenty-five to thirty-five. They might have had a few

speaker: GI
words: more once in a while.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go clear through the eighth?

speaker: GI
words: Yes.

speaker: Ss
words: What do you think of how good learning you got in school?

speaker: GI
words: It was amazing. "These school districts were poor and most

speaker: GI
words: of the teachers they had were people who had gone to their first year

speaker: GI
words: or half a year or something. Lot of them came from Lewiston Normal.

speaker: GI
words: Some of them- once in a while we had one just went to summer school

speaker: GI
words: and took examination to get thejcertificate and some of 'em one year

speaker: GI
words: and some of 'em two years. Think a lot of 'em it was their first

speaker: GI
words: school and they didn't get too good a pay. But I think it is amazing

speaker: GI
words: that a kid could learn as much as they did, and I think one reason

speaker: GI
words: why they did, the whole eight grades and a recitation

speaker: GI
words: bench up in front, you know, and you listened to the other classes

speaker: GI
words: all the way through clear to the eighth grade and you couldn't

speaker: GI
words: help but listen.

speaker: SS
words: It's really get drilled into you.

speaker: GI
words: I think probably that was one reason why some of the oneroom schools

speaker: GI
words: were so successful, I think^because of it. AHearing this over and

speaker: GI
words: over all these years going through the grade school.

speaker: SS
words: When you were a little kid going to school there, do you remember

speaker: SS
words: there being any real big kids that hadn't had much learning?

speaker: GI
words: I remember three boys that were in the eighth grade and they were six

speaker: GI
words: foot tall, great big guys and they used to really give us little kids

speaker: GI
words: the devil. I don't think they ever did finish the eighth grade, but

speaker: GI
words: they had to go to - their parents would send them til one would get

speaker: GI
words: through the eighth grade, so these big guys like that— that was the

speaker: GI
words: year I went to school, they would work in the fall and then come

speaker: GI
words: and go to school in the winter and then/quit in the spring. So they

speaker: GI
words: actually never did- but their folks kept wanting them to get past that

speaker: GI
words: eighth grade examination. And boy, they were mean to us little kids

speaker: GI
words: sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like they weren't  if they were big, picking

speaker: SS
words: on little kids.

speaker: GI
words: They were kids. I remember one time one of 'em Aung me up in a

speaker: GI
words: sack behind a horse out in the barn. A lot of the kids rode horse

speaker: GI
words: back because they had to come quite a ways to school and they had a

speaker: GI
words: barn there by the schoolhouse where they'd keep their horses. I

speaker: GI
words: never will forget that I guess. I was scared to beat the band.

speaker: S
words: He Juing you in a sack—

speaker: GI
words: A gunny sack- hung me there for a little while back behind a horse.

speaker: GI
words: They had a lot of fun but I was so scared, but they didn't leave me

speaker: GI
words: there long.

speaker: SS
words: How old were these kids? Maybe about eighteen?

speaker: GI
words: I imagine, seventeen or eighteen.

speaker: SS
words: It must be kind of embarrassing to them too- you know, they shouldn't

speaker: SS
words: have been in school anymore.

speaker: GI
words: Oh, it was sort of bad for them because actually they were bigger than

speaker: GI
words: the teacher and she had quite a time with those boys sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: Did she have trouble with disclipine and order-

speaker: GI
words: With those older ones. Some of those gals that taught, she could

speaker: GI
words: straighten 'em out, I mean, these bigger kids. I think those big

speaker: GI
words: kids, as I remember, those three boys I first spoke of whenl was in

speaker: GI
words: the first grade were the three biggest because eventually I got to

speaker: GI
words: be one of those big boy, when I got to be in the eighth grade, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Did your mother not teach during that whole time that she was there?

speaker: GI
words: No, she quit teaching when she got married, when she married Dad.

speaker: GI
words: She had taught three other schools on this Ridge prior to her marrying

speaker: GI
words: my dad. She was a schoolteacher so she saw that we studied our lessons

speaker: GI
words: We had to study every night at home after we got our chores done and

speaker: GI
words: our supper eaten.

speaker: SS
words: So, she met your father while she was teaching? Did she know him—?

speaker: GI
words: She met him while she was teaching. She lived on Little Bear Ridge

speaker: GI
words: across the canyon from Big Bear. And I don't think he really knew

speaker: GI
words: her until she came over there teaching. He might have known who

speaker: GI
words: she was. Her name was and they lived on the point

speaker: GI
words: of Little Bear Ridge, but he never really knew her until she- never

speaker: GI
words: went with her or anything till she got to teaching over there.

speaker: GI
words: I did ask you if your father worked hard- I should ask you how hard

speaker: GI
words: you think she worked.

speaker: GI
words: SS

speaker: GI
words: She worked hard. She always canned fruit and we always had a big

speaker: GI
words: job

speaker: GI
words: garden. That was one of us boys was hoeing the garden. I

speaker: GI
words: remember this. This was sort of an incident about my brother. We

speaker: GI
words: used to have to heo in the garden and he'd do something and she'd

speaker: GI
words: kick him out of the garden. And one day it rained a little bit and

speaker: GI
words: Dad was out with us boys hoeing the garden. First thing my brother

speaker: GI
words: did was chop off a stock of corn and he bawled him out and said if he

speaker: GI
words: did that again he'd sure give him a good tanning. He said, "

speaker: GI
words: you going to kick me out of the garden?" Says, "That's what Mom does

speaker: GI
words: when I do something like that." But she worked hard, she made butter

speaker: GI
words: from the cream that we got from the cows and did lots of canning and

speaker: GI
words: she was a pretty good seamstress. She made clothes for us, too. My

speaker: GI
words: sisters, she always made all their dresses and so forth. She was a

speaker: GI
words: very good seamstress.

speaker: SS
words: Did she ever go back to teaching?

speaker: GI
words: No. She was called in a few times, you know to substitute but she

speaker: GI
words: never went back to teaching. She always wanted to but she

speaker: GI
words: always had plenty of work to do at home.

speaker: SS
words: Wasn't that pretty much the pattern of young women in those days?

speaker: SS
words: They'd teach and then they'd marry and then that was it.

speaker: GI
words: That was it, yeah. Very few of 'em went back to school. Some of

speaker: GI
words: 'em would after their family was grown or something, maybe. But

speaker: GI
words: generally they stayed home and took care of the- ran the household

speaker: GI
words: and took care of the family. Seemed to be the pattern in those days.

speaker: SS
words: For you to get to school in Kendrick; was that a hardship? After

speaker: SS
words: you got out of the eighth grade?

speaker: GI
words: After I got out of the eighth grade; first year I rode horseback to

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick in the fall and then in the spring when the weather was good

speaker: GI
words: and then in the wintertime S stayed in town. Then the second year I

speaker: GI
words: was big enough- I was fourteen or so—fstarted driving a car. Drove a

speaker: GI
words: car in the fall and spring. I drove down that old grade and up it

speaker: GI
words: when it was put it in low gear and stayAin low gear all the way till

speaker: GI
words: you got to the top. Three mile grade coming up. But I guess,

speaker: GI
words: pcobably- I did it a little bit when I was a freshman in high school

speaker: GI
words: but when the weather got rainy and slick, then I rode horseback. Then

speaker: GI
words: after that first year we never did ride horseback again, we always

speaker: GI
words: drove in the fall and then we stayed in town in the wintertime.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a second car?

speaker: GI
words: No, that was the family car. It was an old 1920 Dodge. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Was that grade ever dangerous- real dangerous to be on? I've driven

speaker: SS
words: it now, I've wondered in the wintertime-

speaker: GI
words: You haven't driven that old grade.

speaker: SS
words: No, I drive the one off of Little Bear Ridge to Kendrick.

speaker: GI
words: This one was on the other side and it was dangerous. I guess you can

speaker: GI
words: get up and down^now because some people live part way up on it, but

speaker: GI
words: it was really a dangerous grade. Now, you take a dangerous grade

speaker: GI
words: then- you had to be used to driving on it. Once in a while there

speaker: GI
words: was a turnout where you could see somebody coming, you'd have to wait

speaker: GI
words: to pass. People that drove it all-time were pretty good about thatthey

speaker: GI
words: see somebody coming and they were close to the turnout, they'd

speaker: GI
words: wait or vice versa. Sometimes you'd have to back up, but it was a

speaker: GI
words: pretty dangerous grade, really. You'd look right straight down in the

speaker: GI
words: canyon.

speaker: SS
words: In the winter,say, how did you handle it when there was snow on top

speaker: SS
words: and nothing down in Kendrick? You couldn't really take a sled down

speaker: SS
words: there in the early days all the way if there was no snow in Kendrick.

speaker: GI
words: No, it was pretty rough. Seems like we used to have more snow then,

speaker: GI
words: and there was another grade on the other side , called the Pine Creek

speaker: GI
words: grade that they go down in the winter and they'd leave their sled

speaker: GI
words: at the bottom of the grade and go to town.

speaker: SS
words: Walk in?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, or take one of the horses and ride in. But lots of times used

speaker: GI
words: a buggy and they'd get the road broke and they'd use a buggy on top,

speaker: GI
words: cut the tracks, if they couldn't get to town with a sled. When I was

speaker: GI
words: in high school lots of times we didn't think anything about when Fri

speaker: GI
words: day night when school was out,alk home. It would kill kids now to

speaker: GI
words: walk that eight or nine miles! It was about eight miles or eight and

speaker: GI
words: a half-miles, I guess, home. Lots of times we'd walk down Sunday

speaker: GI
words: night. And we didn't think anything about that then.

speaker: SS
words: You never stayed in town except in the winter?

speaker: GI
words: Wintertime was all.

speaker: SS
words: Walking nine miles, it took quite a while.

speaker: GI
words: We used to walk to the top of the grade and we'd take a shortcut-

speaker: GI
words: We'd run down the grade, we d make it in an hour and twenty minutes,

speaker: GI
words: probably. (Chuckles) Sometimes the folks would take us to the top of

speaker: GI
words: the grade and there was two shortcuts on the grade where we could not

speaker: GI
words: follow the road and go straight down the hill and we'd take the shortcuts

speaker: GI
words: but coming back up, when we'd walk home, the shortcuts were

speaker: GI
words: steeper, we couldn't go too fast.

speaker: SS
words: What about the transition for you from the country school to the Ken

speaker: SS
words: drick school? Was it a big change?

speaker: GI
words: Yes, it was. It didn't take me too long to get oriented, but it was

speaker: GI
words: quite a change at first. You was used to the small group and so forth.

speaker: GI
words: I sure felt like a big pebble in a lake but it didn t take me long to

speaker: GI
words: get oriented.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard people talk about how getting into a town and mixing with

speaker: SS
words: the town kids, they really feel backwards at first, because the town

speaker: SS
words: kids are a little more slick. The big difference seemed to be in

speaker: SS
words: Moscow and the people out of town. Did you feel that?

speaker: GI
words: I felt that at first, but, I don't know, I was pretty easy to make

speaker: GI
words: friends and I got along real good with the town kids. They ^ed to

speaker: GI
words: call us country jerks and that kind of stuff, but it didn't take long.

speaker: GI
words: With Kendrick there was so many of them that came in from the rural

speaker: GI
words: areas, too, anyway, so there was a lot of country kids, so actually,

speaker: GI
words: I imagine the percent was probably 50-50 so it didn't take very long

speaker: GI
words: to get to knowing each other and working together.

speaker: SS
words: When you were in high school, was it mostly just studies or was there

speaker: SS
words: a lot of, what today seems to be so big in high school, sports and

speaker: SS
words: extracurricular activities.

speaker: GI
words: Well, we had- Basically, the main thing was studies. We had

speaker: GI
words: basketball and baseball and we never had football in Kendrick until

speaker: GI
words: the year after I went out; got out of high school. I graduated in

speaker: GI
words: the spring of '28 and they never started football until that fall,

speaker: GI
words: but we had baseball and basketball. I played basketball, I wasn't

speaker: GI
words: much for baseball, but I was on the basketball team for three years,

speaker: GI
words: I guess when I was in high school.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of socializing between the boys and the girls when you

speaker: SS
words: were growing up? Must opportunity for it?

speaker: GI
words: Well, we- quite a little, I guess, called it. We used to have

speaker: GI
words: in our local community up there, every winter, we used to have what

speaker: GI
words: they called Literary Society. And every Saturday night or every other

speaker: GI
words: Saturday night certain groups would be in charge of putting on a pro

speaker: GI
words: gram for that evening and then they'd have their eats and stuff af

speaker: GI
words: terwards, and then used to have these old basket suppers.

speaker: GI
words: Auction off boxes and pie suppers, auction off pies and stuff like that

speaker: GI
words: to money for different things in the community. Then we always

speaker: GI
words: had I remember, we always had a big deal at Christmastime. Used

speaker: GI
words: to have what they called UB church, was just a little ways, United

speaker: GI
words: Brethern Church, from the schoolhouse. All the schools would go to

speaker: GI
words: gether and put on the Christmas porgram. And they would go around

speaker: GI
words: the community and collect money to buy treats, so everybody got a

speaker: GI
words: sack of candy. And every school would be in charge of putting on a

speaker: GI
words: part of the program. Have some dialogues and have some speeches and

speaker: GI
words: so forth, and it was really quite a festive occasion, that Christmasthe

speaker: GI
words: old community Christmas tree.

speaker: SS
words: When you had like these literaries and that kind of stuff, did you

speaker: SS
words: have dances after them?

speaker: GI
words: Dancing was taboo. We could play Skip-to-my-Lou and that kind of

speaker: GI
words: stuff, but dancing, that was sort of bad for some of 'em, so they

speaker: GI
words: could play games, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a divided opinion at the time, some were for it and some not?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yeah. And so they just generally just compromised.

speaker: SS
words: So you played these dancing and singing games? Party games?

speaker: GI
words: Party games. I used to think it sort of funny, they could play

speaker: GI
words: Skip-to-my-Lou, but they couldn't dance! But later on, in the early

speaker: GI
words: '20's they built a community hall there and they used to have public

speaker: GI
words: dances over there. And then after they got that hall they used to

speaker: GI
words: have their Christmas tree in the community hall. It's still standing

speaker: GI
words: there, in fact, it's on one of the places I.

speaker: SS
words: That hall got a pretty good sized draw, too. Heard people from Bovill

speaker: SS
words: say they went over there.

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yeah, those public dances, they used to come from all over. They

speaker: GI
words: used to have some real whingdings there.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I heard. (Chuckles)

speaker: GI
words: My folks wouldn't let me go to the dances. We could go to other things

speaker: GI
words: over there. But my dad, he was sort of against dancing. I never got

speaker: GI
words: to until I to Then I took dancing lessons and started dancing, but he was real strict

speaker: GI
words: about dancing. And he was sort of

speaker: GI
words: strict about cards. When we were younger, we couldn't play even pino

speaker: GI
words: chle or something, but anyway after we got up in high school, we played

speaker: GI
words: anyway and he accepted it. But he " that dancing and cards and

speaker: GI
words: drinking and all that kind of stuff ran together.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think for him that that was a religious belief, or was it more

speaker: SS
words: just morals?

speaker: GI
words: Well, religious and moral, I suppose. His mother was real strict, I

speaker: GI
words: guess and she died when he was a teenager, and I guess she left quite

speaker: GI
words: an impression on him. He was just that way. pother was a lot

speaker: GI
words: more broadminded about it, but he was pretty strict.

speaker: SS
words: So, he never had a drink?

speaker: GI
words: He never drank at all. He was a teetotler.

speaker: SS
words: He must have been for Prohibition, when it came in.

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yeah. He wasn't one of the real rabid ones who would go out and

speaker: GI
words: preach it. But if you was with him and wanted a drink, he'd let you

speaker: GI
words: drink, but he just didn't do it himself.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that the dancehall on the Ridge when it came in caused some

speaker: SS
words: conflict with the people who were really against dancing.

speaker: GI
words: Yes, it did and still, on the other hand, they actually got so they

speaker: GI
words: had Sunday School in that community hall part of the time. They had

speaker: GI
words: Sunday School next morning after they had a dance there on Saturday

speaker: GI
words: night. So each group learned to live with it. And it was pretty fun

speaker: GI
words: ny in the local community as I look back, they'd fight like cats and

speaker: GI
words: dogs if somebody'd have bad luck or out or something of

speaker: GI
words: trouble then all the neighbors would come in and help. Forget all their

speaker: GI
words: petty peeves and work for each other. And when they built this com

speaker: GI
words: munity hall it was a donation of labor and every body helped. I know

speaker: GI
words: my dad helped work on it, too. I guess he sort of had the attitude,

speaker: GI
words: if the people wanted to do that, it was alright, but he just didn't

speaker: GI
words: do it himself and he didn't like his family to be doing some of those

speaker: GI
words: things, like drinking and dancing.

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that it was pretty much divided into two groups, those

speaker: SS
words: that accepted drinking and dancing and those that were against it?

speaker: SS
words: Is that pretty much the way it was

speaker: GI
words: I guess it was, but still-

speaker: SS
words: It is common in a small community.

speaker: GI
words: Yes, I imagine so. I guess it was. Some of these people that drank

speaker: GI
words: and tore around would come to church Sunday, too. It wasn't Straight

speaker: GI
words: down the line.

speaker: SS
words: I imagine there were some people that believed in dancing and not in

speaker: SS
words: drinking,

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, you're right.

speaker: SS
words: What about the community church, did most of the people from the area

speaker: SS
words: go to that church?

speaker: GI
words: Well, they had two churches. The one on the other end of the Ridge

speaker: GI
words: was the Lutheran. Most of the people at one time lived on the lower

speaker: GI
words: end of the Ridge were Lutherans and they had their Bethany Lutheran

speaker: GI
words: Church and then I think most of the rest of the people, even they did

speaker: GI
words: belong to other denominations would come to church there and take part

speaker: GI
words: in Sunday School, because it was a church. Later on that church was-

speaker: GI
words: The United Brether n wanted to sell it, it sort of died out and they

speaker: GI
words: couldn't furnish the minister therf. grandfather and my dad and

speaker: GI
words: my uncle bought the building and my grandfather donated the land and

speaker: GI
words: so the community tore it down and built the church they have there by

speaker: GI
words: the cemetery from the old lumber, of course, with some new, but bas

speaker: GI
words: ically the building was built with the timbers and stuff from

speaker: GI
words: church.

speaker: SS
words: And now it's a community church?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, it's not used very much. Once in a while they have funerals,

speaker: GI
words: once in a while they have a service there, but there's not near the

speaker: GI
words: people there used to be here. Used to have quite a group come. Used

speaker: GI
words: to have regular revival meetings and that type of thing there. Well,

speaker: GI
words: I guess they did after they first built the church, but the old church

speaker: GI
words: used to have quite a lot of that, and they had some of that after the

speaker: GI
words: new church was built. That was moved there in the '30's.

speaker: SS
words: Would the revivals last very long?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, they'd have two weeks.

speaker: SS
words: Were they well attended?

speaker: GI
words: pretty well. I remember we used to go. When we was kids we used to

speaker: GI
words: walk down to that church. It was a little over a mile from our place 4~o

speaker: GI
words: the old church, and we used to walk to church every night in the win

speaker: GI
words: tertime.

speaker: SS
words: What do you remember what the actual revival itself was like? Was

speaker: SS
words: the preaching pretty strong?

speaker: GI
words: Well, as I remember it was, seemed like. Yeah, I think it was pret

speaker: GI
words: ty sincere and strong. Affected some people, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Because I've heard that the way it affected a lot of people was very

speaker: SS
words: emotional.

speaker: GI
words: They still have that, you have your emotional groups, too, I mean

speaker: GI
words: some churches are a lot more emotional than others, butA had the

speaker: GI
words: whole bunch. There was some of 'em that were affected a lot more

speaker: GI
words: than others. Then we used to have the^church parties, too. That was

speaker: GI
words: part of the community deal sometimes, too.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of parties?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, that'd just be a church dinner or something. We call

speaker: GI
words: 'em bazaars now, stuff like that. Get together and maybe on a Sunday

speaker: GI
words: have a church dinner, something like that.

speaker: SS
words: Were most of the people expected to go to church on Sunday?

speaker: GI
words: It seemen.to me like the biggest part of 'em at that time went to

speaker: GI
words: church sometime. But we had pretty good attendance, as I remember as

speaker: GI
words: a kid.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe not every Sunday though. What about working on Sunday? Were

speaker: SS
words: there a lot of people that wouldn't do any work on Sunday?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yeah. There was a lot of people who wouldn't do anything on Sun

speaker: GI
words: day. Even people that didn't go to church, wouldn't work on Sunday.

speaker: GI
words: There was a few that worked on Sunday. That was one thing that my

speaker: GI
words: dad always said, "There was one day for rest." And we never did work

speaker: GI
words: in the field on Sunday around our home. Of course, we had the chores

speaker: GI
words: and stuff like that to do, but going out and actually doing work-in

speaker: GI
words: the field, we never did do that. He always said we'd have one day off.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a visiting day for you?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, used to go visit the neighbors and we used to have family pic

speaker: GI
words: nics in the summertime. It was a day that either you had company or

speaker: GI
words: went someplace and was company for somebody else. Seemtf like

speaker: GI
words: a lot more of that than there is now.

speaker: SS
words: When you say family picnic, that makes me wonder, too; when you so

speaker: SS
words: cialized then was it mostly with the family?

speaker: GI
words: Big part of it. My uncles lived there and my grandmother was there

speaker: GI
words: and my dad's sister married Amos Moore and they had a family and they

speaker: GI
words: lived close there and we visitA back and forth among ourselves,

speaker: GI
words: but still on the other hand, too, some of the neighbors, we'd be in

speaker: GI
words: vited there for dinner on Sunday or go someplace else on Sunday. But

speaker: GI
words: I imagine that nearly half of it was among the family itself, or rela

speaker: GI
words: tives. We had some relatives lived in Moscow and some lived at Col

speaker: GI
words: fax and then after we got using automobiles we used to visit with

speaker: GI
words: them on Sundays and they'd visit us. A big part of it was family.

speaker: SS
words: All these relatives; had more than one family come here originally

speaker: SS
words: that was related?

speaker: GI
words: See, my grandfather came here- when he came he brought his hired man

speaker: GI
words: with him and his hired man homesteaded the same as he did, and that

speaker: GI
words: family grew up being pretty close. And then my aunt married one of

speaker: GI
words: the neighbor boys when she was pretty young. She was about ten or

speaker: GI
words: twelve when she came here and I guess she got married when she was

speaker: GI
words: sixteen, so that stErted in with another family, so it didn't take

speaker: GI
words: long. And then my Grandmother Ingle's brother lived in- had three

speaker: GI
words: brothers lived in Colfax and^another brother lived- no, cousin-

speaker: GI
words: A brother lived at Colfax and then the cousins- there were three

speaker: GI
words: of 'em lived at Colfax and one lived in Deary and one of 'em lived

speaker: GI
words: on the upper end of the ridge. And that came about^ my Grandmother

speaker: GI
words: Ingle's side. We had those relatives to visit with and they had families

speaker: GI
words: too.

speaker: SS
words: Your grandfather; where did he come from?

speaker: GI
words: He came from originally Tennessee and then he went to Illinois and he

speaker: GI
words: was in Illinois when he actually came to Idaho. He came out, I guess,

speaker: GI
words: in the fall of 1883. I think it was '83. At that time they had the

speaker: GI
words: railroad built as far as Colfax. And they rented a car on the rail

speaker: GI
words: road and piled all their belongings on one end and the family lived

speaker: GI
words: in one end of it while they moved out on the railroad. The story that

speaker: GI
words: I always heard him say that he and his hired man was with him, he

speaker: GI
words: came at the same time, brought his family with him. He had a wife

speaker: GI
words: and some children and grandfather had three or four children at that

speaker: GI
words: time. Some say he - they rented horses and rode over there and found

speaker: GI
words: this land where we are and others said he rented a buggy, so I don't

speaker: GI
words: know which he did, but anyway, he got from Colfax over there and they

speaker: GI
words: settled0on Big Bear Ridge.

speaker: SS
words: And he was a homesteader? Nobody else had been on the place?

speaker: GI
words: No. Well, first he had a right for a preemption and he went on the

speaker: GI
words: preemption first and then this place that he homesteaded, somebody had

speaker: GI
words: squatted on it and then gave it up so then he- it was open, so my

speaker: GI
words: grandfather filed a homestead on it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he did it that way because he still wanted to have his

speaker: SS
words: right to homestead?

speaker: GI
words: Probably. I don't know what the deal was.

speaker: SS
words: Did he get the preemption too?

speaker: GI
words: Yes. Proved up on both of them. Then my uncle, the oldest one of

speaker: GI
words: the family, there was a piece of land just north of grandfather's

speaker: GI
words: preemption that hadn't been taken when he got of age, so he home

speaker: GI
words: steaded that. My cousin has that place and the one that my grandfather

speaker: GI
words: s old preemption. He it now.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hear from him why he left Tennessee?

speaker: GI
words: Yes. He had asthma awful bad. And I guess he just had an awful time.

speaker: GI
words: And somebody told him if he'd go West he wouldn't have it, so he just

speaker: GI
words: bundled his family up and decided to try it. I remember him saying

speaker: GI
words: he never had asthma all his life after he got out here. Remember him

speaker: GI
words: saying as soon as he came over the mountains he started to breathe.

speaker: GI
words: And I think that was one of the things that brought him out here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have an idea of about how old he was when he came West?

speaker: GI
words: Well, I could figure it out. He was eighty-four years old when he

speaker: GI
words: died and he died in 1934.

speaker: SS
words: So, he would have been about thirty-two. You said he came in '82?

speaker: GI
words: '83. he was a reasonably young man.

speaker: SS
words: Did he talk to you at all about what those early pioneer conditions

speaker: SS
words: were?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes. After he moved down to this other place; they built a house

speaker: GI
words: and he moved down there and he had his garden and he us ed to have us

speaker: GI
words: boys help put his wood in and he'd come up to the place everyday.

speaker: GI
words: He'd walk across the field up to our house. He visited with me a

speaker: GI
words: lot. Us kids, I mean. He used to sit and talk with us. It was really

speaker: GI
words: interesting some of the things.

speaker: SS
words: How hard did he make it out to be when it was just-\hese first settlers?

speaker: GI
words: Pretty rough. I remember him saying the first year when he came here

speaker: GI
words: in the fall and I guess it was '83, and they didn't have much to eat

speaker: GI
words: and the neighbors had lots of rutabagas and they killed a bear, and

speaker: GI
words: he said they lived on rutabagas and bear meat, of co urse, they got

speaker: GI
words: a deer I guess too ne said it was a pretty tough winter. I guess they

speaker: GI
words: completely ran out of groceries early in the spring and still had a

speaker: GI
words: lot of snow. There was one trip they fixed up an old bobsled; took

speaker: GI
words: the front runners off of the bobsled and put a team on it, put a box

speaker: GI
words: on it, just had one set of runners and c to Moscow so they could

speaker: GI
words: get some groceries. That was the nearest place they had to come to

speaker: GI
words: get groceries. Took 'em two days, to come to Moscow.. Anyway, when

speaker: GI
words: he first came they used to, every fall, after they got established,

speaker: GI
words: they'd bring the team and wagon and try to leave early in the morning

speaker: GI
words: and get to Moscow and get their winter's supply. And they used

speaker: GI
words: to try to get out of here in time to get their buying done that day

speaker: GI
words: and that night they'd hitch up and go out to what's called Corwall

speaker: GI
words: out here and there was a family name of Hobart that lived out there

speaker: GI
words: that were real good friends and they'd always go out there and camp

speaker: GI
words: the rest of that night and then drive back home the n ext day. I've

speaker: GI
words: heard him talk about that several times; how they used to visit with

speaker: GI
words: old Sam Hobart.

speaker: SS
words: Did they camp out? Outside of the house?

speaker: GI
words: Yes. I guess he'd take as many of 'em inside as he could. But that

speaker: GI
words: was the stopping place for the people.

speaker: SS
words: Well, this kind of deal was just in the fall?

speaker: GI
words: Well, that was the fall deal.^ They always made that one trip to town.

speaker: GI
words: Sometimes they got so they made two trips to Moscow a year, but that

speaker: GI
words: was the big one but that was in the fall to get their winter supplies.

speaker: GI
words: They'd get everything fixed up and they had their food canned from

speaker: GI
words: garden and their potatoes dug in the cellar and they were just

speaker: GI
words: about holed up for the winter, the way he used to tell it.

speaker: SS
words: I've often wondered about how much turnover there was among these

speaker: SS
words: real earliest pioneers, because they say what happened a lot of places

speaker: SS
words: that you read about, is that there was a lot of turnover. And maybe

speaker: SS
words: one in ten actually wound up staying. And yet most people seemed to -

speaker: SS
words: those people that stayed seemed to remember it as a lot more stable.

speaker: SS
words: And I just wondered,

speaker: GI
words: I think that maybe the population was pretty stable there for several

speaker: GI
words: years, because there's two of us old families that's still out there

speaker: GI
words: now. The Moreys; Mrs. Cecil Chamberlin was one of the Moreys; their

speaker: GI
words: place joined ours. And their father came and homesteaded a year or

speaker: GI
words: two or three before my father homesteaded there. I think there was

speaker: GI
words: quite a few of 'em stayed, maybe when you look back there wasn't too

speaker: GI
words: big a percent, but I imagine 50-60% of 'em stayed several years, just

speaker: GI
words: from listening to my father and grandfather talking.

speaker: SS
words: I was thinking that maybe some of these real early people, say like

speaker: SS
words: the guy that wassquatting on this place, it might have been a lot of

speaker: SS
words: people like that right at the very beginning, that they were the ones

speaker: SS
words: that turnedpver.

speaker: GI
words: Of course, that probably made a lot of turnover. I think he just

speaker: GI
words: came there and he put up a shack and he was going to homestead on

speaker: GI
words: it, prove up on it, and he just decided it was too hard so then he

speaker: GI
words: pulls out and my grandfather it up, and I think there were

speaker: GI
words: several places like that. Maybe that caused a lot of the moving, people

speaker: GI
words: would decide it was going to be too hard a deal and leave.

speaker: SS
words: In the winters, when you were a kid, did things get really slow? Was

speaker: SS
words: there a lot of time that your parents couldn't be that busy? Or were

speaker: SS
words: they really busy in the winter, too?

speaker: GI
words: Well, they always had livestock that they had to take care of. Dad

speaker: GI
words: used to raise hogs and he used to have quite a few of 'em, and later

speaker: GI
words: when I was a kid they had these Potlatch Forest lumbercamps; one

speaker: GI
words: just south of Deary and they weren't too far off and he used to butcher

speaker: GI
words: hogs in the wintertime and deliver butchered hogs to these lumbercamps.

speaker: GI
words: So he kept pretty busy. Of course, it not have been that way with

speaker: GI
words: some of the families, but I remember he used to do lots of butchering

speaker: GI
words: in the wintertime. He used to butcher every two weeks, probably.

speaker: GI
words: Whenever you butchered in those days, the neighbors all came in and

speaker: GI
words: help you butcher and you'd go help them butcher. I remember he used

speaker: GI
words: to butcher several hogs.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the pace was about the same as the rest of the year, or

speaker: SS
words: would you say it would be more leisurely?

speaker: SS
words: I think they didn't near as fast as we do. They kept busy and

speaker: SS
words: they made wood; some of 'em made wood in the winter and some of 'em

speaker: SS
words: made posts. And they had their winter jobs that they did in the win

speaker: SS
words: ter. I don't think they hit it quite as hard as we do now.

speaker: SS
words: Did he do a lot of repair work in the winter then?

speaker: GI
words: Some. They didn't have shops on farms^like they do now. And it wasn t

speaker: GI
words: too much to fix up a gangplow or a harrow drawbar and stuff like that.

speaker: GI
words: It didn't take very long to do the repair on machinery. Of course,

speaker: GI
words: the binders, when they got those, there was repair, but a binder's

speaker: GI
words: small compared to a combine.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much concern in your family; was he very concerned about

speaker: SS
words: the prospects of being able to make good on the place? I'm wondering

speaker: SS
words: how close to the bone it was. Like I've heard after the First World

speaker: SS
words: War there was a pretty bad falling off in prices and things were pret

speaker: SS
words: ty tough.

speaker: GI
words: Well, it was pretty rough. There during the Depression and three

speaker: GI
words: of us kids in college and he'd gotten up and he'd been on this

speaker: GI
words: bankA directors and he got to be president of the bank and

speaker: GI
words: the bank closed and I think he only had $75 to his name. And that

speaker: GI
words: was pretty tough going, I tell you. And I quit school that year the

speaker: GI
words: last sememster and went home and we lined up all the beef cows we

speaker: GI
words: could and milk cows and at that time we had a creamery, cream station,

speaker: GI
words: in Kendrick and sold cream; and it was pretty rough times then. But

speaker: GI
words: he always knew it was going to be better someday. I always remember

speaker: GI
words: my uncle, he aways said, "Oh, the country's going to the devil."

speaker: GI
words: "Look here Amy." And Dad would say, "It's not that bad"! And Dad always figured it

speaker: GI
words: was going to get better.

speaker: SS
words: What did you call him?

speaker: GI
words: His name was Amos,.that was his brother-in-law and he called him Amy.

speaker: SS
words: Probably never did convince him, though.

speaker: GI
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: What was it that did happen in the bank? What kind of situation was

speaker: SS
words: it that developed?

speaker: GI
words: What happened was when this depression came and people got scared and

speaker: GI
words: they started drawing their money out and the banks had loans on it;

speaker: GI
words: had the money loaned out and they couldn't liquidate fast enough to

speaker: GI
words: pay off the depositors. That's what closed the banks in the depres

speaker: GI
words: sion then. If they'd have just left their money in the bank and

speaker: GI
words: stayed with them, wouldn't have any trouble, but you can see what

speaker: GI
words: happens; you got a loan out here say that's due in six months and the

speaker: GI
words: guy comes in and wants his money and you can't liquidate and that

speaker: GI
words: guy can't dig up the money right now, so that's what happens to the

speaker: GI
words: bank. People just pulling their savings out of the banks, afraid

speaker: GI
words: the bank's going to go broke. It's like in the Depression; it got

speaker: GI
words: to be a chain thing. One bank goes broke and everybody rushed into

speaker: GI
words: the next bank and other banks and start drawing their money out.

speaker: SS
words: Which bank was this?

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick State Bank.

speaker: SS
words: Did it close down before the bank holiday? Was it before Roosevelt

speaker: SS
words: got in?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah. They opened it again later on and then the two banks went to

speaker: GI
words: gether and made one bank. But it closed about a month before that

speaker: GI
words: bank holiday.

speaker: SS
words: What were the losses at the point that it closed?

speaker: GI
words: Well, actually, it came out pretty good. Of course it had to be

speaker: GI
words: liquidated, but they liquidated out nearly 100%. They had good paper,

speaker: GI
words: good mortgage and good notes and stuff like that, it was just the

speaker: GI
words: thing that you just can't liquidate^fast enough when the people draw

speaker: GI
words: their money out. It took over a period of years, but they pretty

speaker: GI
words: well liquidated out.

speaker: SS
words: Does that mean that people that owed money paid it back?

speaker: GI
words: Right. They way they got it started again, they got some of the large

speaker: GI
words: stockholders that had, they called it big money then, $7,000, $8,000

speaker: GI
words: to go in and put their money as stock, and they got the thing to going

speaker: GI
words: again that way. And thesexstockholders they came out all right. The

speaker: GI
words: people that did that they came out real good, because later on the bank

speaker: GI
words: was solvent.

speaker: SS
words: How many years later did they start it up again?

speaker: GI
words: It was started up that next year after they closed. I don't think

speaker: GI
words: it was closed six months.

speaker: SS
words: How long did it take your father to get his money bank; what he had

speaker: SS
words: in it?

speaker: GI
words: I think within a year or so they had what he had in it. Well, he lost

speaker: GI
words: some, of course, but not too much.

speaker: SS
words: That sounds like it wasn't too bad then. Like I heard the Moscow State

speaker: SS
words: Bank only paid back something like fifty cents on the dollar.

speaker: GI
words: Well, there was several of them that did, but I think that that one

speaker: GI
words: particular bank, I think most everybody got their money out of it in

speaker: GI
words: the longrun. Pretty sure they did.

speaker: SS
words: It seems to me that that's also what happened in Troy, although the

speaker: SS
words: bank didn't close.

speaker: GI
words: The Troy bank was just about- they just told the depositors if they

speaker: GI
words: drew out their money they wouldn't have a bank and the depositors

speaker: GI
words: stuck with Ole Bdman, the old man that was in there for years, you

speaker: GI
words: know. They were just about ready to close but they kept it open by

speaker: GI
words: talking the people out of taking their money out. And Frank Green

speaker: GI
words: down there help a lot, he was pretty well off.

speaker: SS
words: So you think that if maybe they'd tried a little harder in Kendrick

speaker: SS
words: and if they'd have moved on it faster they could have—

speaker: GI
words: It's hard to combat something like that. One thing that sort of hurt

speaker: GI
words: em^at the Kendrick State Bank; the bank in Juliaetta was owned by

speaker: GI
words: the- well, it's the First Security now, but it was Melgar^ that had

speaker: GI
words: that old bank and they bought that Juliaetta bank and it wasn't going

speaker: GI
words: very good, and those people were the first to pull their money out.

speaker: GI
words: Some of 'em didn't like it they were losing their bank and they

speaker: GI
words: didn't realize what kind of a predicament they were really in. And

speaker: GI
words: some of those people were the first to^uli their money out. So

speaker: SS
words: Out of the Kendrick bank and put it into the Juliaetta bank.

speaker: GI
words: Yes. After the Juliaetta bank was closed, the Kendrick bank bought

speaker: GI
words: the Juliaetta bank, and so that moved all the depositors and all their

speaker: GI
words: paper and so forth. So, when this depression started going and some

speaker: GI
words: of them didn't like losing their bank in Juliaetta, so they were pro

speaker: GI
words: bably the first to start drawing their money out.

speaker: SS
words: Was it the Kandrick State that bought the Juliaetta bank?

speaker: GI
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And it had been Melgafd's?

speaker: GI
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And there were two banks in Kendrick, too?

speaker: GI
words: Right. There was the Farmer's Bank and the Kendrick State. My dad

speaker: GI
words: was a stockholder in the Farmer's Bank, too, but not very much. Just

speaker: GI
words: one share. They were $100 shares in those days, I think it was.

speaker: SS
words: That might have also put more strain on the community resources to

speaker: SS
words: have two banks instead of one.

speaker: GI
words: Right. At one time Troy had two banks, too, but they just had one

speaker: GI
words: bank at this time. That made a difference, too, I'll say it did.

speaker: GI
words: And the other bank was a branch of the- it was a Vollmer bank; branch

speaker: GI
words: of the Vollmer system in Lewis ton. And they had the backingjdown there

speaker: GI
words: of the bigger bank and they kept it from closing it's doors, but it

speaker: GI
words: wasn't in very good shape, either.

speaker: SS
words: Mentioning Vollmer; people in the early days- did Vollmer have kind

speaker: SS
words: of a bad reputation around Kendrick?

speaker: GI
words: Yes, yes, he did. There was some Vollmer land adjoinedJthe place that

speaker: GI
words: I have now and the Vollmer place, the other on the east- west side of

speaker: GI
words: the Ridge, too. I think probably the reason- I don't know how this

speaker: GI
words: happened but undoubtedly they probably loaned people money and then

speaker: GI
words: they couldn't pay their bill and they foreclosed on their land.

speaker: GI
words: And they got a pretty big holding. And probably some of the people

speaker: GI
words: just walked off and left it too, and probably some of them the

speaker: GI
words: closed. I know they foreclosed on one guy that lived over there by

speaker: GI
words: us. And they had an old guy, called him S. D. White, used to be the

speaker: GI
words: for Vollmer and oh, he was hard-boiled. I imagine he was

speaker: GI
words: pretty rough on some of these renters they had.

speaker: SS
words: Did he actually come out to these people's places?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, I used to see him quite often when I was a kid. When they

speaker: GI
words: didn't pay their rent and that kind of stuff. I remember seeing him

speaker: GI
words: a lot. I remember one time that he called my dad up over the phone

speaker: GI
words: and we had^threshers there and they had this big machine threshing

speaker: GI
words: and I remember Dad saying, "You will?" He says, "We're ready to go."

speaker: GI
words: That's all Dad said and I asked him he said and he said that

speaker: GI
words: S. D. White said that he's going to put the h^ole board of directors

speaker: GI
words: of the Roachdale Warehouse in jail because they'd bought some crop

speaker: GI
words: off of one of these places.

speaker: GI
words: And he used to stop and talk to Dad once in a while, they really rib

speaker: GI
words: bed each other. I remember Dad was kidding him about what a hard-boiled

speaker: GI
words: guy he was. I think that's really where he go the reputation from.

speaker: GI
words: This one old guy he was as hard as nails. I suppose in those days

speaker: GI
words: maybe they had to be, I don't know. They wouldn't stay in business

speaker: GI
words: nowdays, I don't think.

speaker: SS
words: When you were growing up then, do you have any memories of Prohibi

speaker: SS
words: tion and moonshiners?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes, we had moonshine. There was moonshine a lot when I was a

speaker: GI
words: kid, and I remember them talking about it a lot. They used to catch

speaker: GI
words: a still once in a while out in that wheat country, down in the woods

speaker: GI
words: someplace.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father, being opposed to drinking, make you feel that you

speaker: SS
words: couldn't try it?

speaker: GI
words: Well, I don't know. I never had any desire to try it. Lot of the

speaker: GI
words: kids did. I've seen lots of it but I just never wanted to try it.

speaker: SS
words: Then your father's teaching-

speaker: GI
words: Must have been. Must have been his teaching.

speaker: SS
words: How much opportunity was there, do you think for the kids that were

speaker: SS
words: growing up to stay around Kendrick and the Ridge? With these big

speaker: SS
words: families and small farms.

speaker: GI
words: Well, really wasn't too much. Way it seemed to work out, the kids

speaker: GI
words: that went on to schoolJKon to college never came back, but the ones

speaker: GI
words: that never went to school after the high school and some of them after

speaker: GI
words: the eighth grade all stayed around home. Stayed in the community

speaker: GI
words: until they'd find something else, maybe get into some other field,

speaker: GI
words: but there wasn't too much work for big employment in the area. Of

speaker: GI
words: course, a lot of 'em went to the timber work, too. So they'd leave

speaker: GI
words: home to go work in the woods, the Potlatch Forest or some of those

speaker: GI
words: camps down at Deary and Bovill, and some of them went there. But stayed

speaker: GI
words: right in their own community. There wasn't much for them to do un

speaker: GI
words: less they farmed. I know there was one family there that all thevr

speaker: GI
words: kids stayed on the farm and they'd watch and maybe they'd get to rent

speaker: GI
words: a farm and get married and live there.

speaker: SS
words: At your time, did most of the kids go on to high school from the eighth

speaker: SS
words: grade?

speaker: GI
words: It was a small percent, really. And college, boy, that was out of

speaker: GI
words: this world! I think out of high school wasn't very big,

speaker: GI
words: seven pupils graduated from high school the same year I did, I think

speaker: GI
words: I was the only one that went on to college. Let's see, one of 'em

speaker: GI
words: went to business college, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Were you set on going to college? Was it your idea or your father's?

speaker: GI
words: I just grew up with the idea that I wanted to go to college and tiy

speaker: GI
words: mother had been a school teacher and I guess it was just in my mind

speaker: GI
words: as I grew up, I just naturally supposed that was the thing that I

speaker: GI
words: wanted to do. And there were four of us and we all went to college.

speaker: GI
words: I was the only one that never graduated. I got my four years in. I

speaker: GI
words: quit the midyear in '32 when the Depression went home. And

speaker: GI
words: then I came back in the fall of '33, and so I got my number of years

speaker: GI
words: in but never got the right semesters in. But my brother went on and

speaker: GI
words: graduated from Ag school and then he came back and he and I farmed

speaker: GI
words: together and then he went back and got his master's degree in soil

speaker: GI
words: And my sister, one of em was a commercial teacher, taught school

speaker: GI
words: seven years and the other sister was a home ec graduate. She taught

speaker: GI
words: two or three years before she got married and then they went to New

speaker: GI
words: Mexico andA work(for the Indian Service and so she taught home

speaker: GI
words: ec down in- was it Albuquerque?- no, it wasn't Albuquerque but Gallup.

speaker: GI
words: She taught home ec there several years. We sort of had some

speaker: GI
words: resentment toward us kids in the local community about us going on to

speaker: GI
words: school with certain people, but we never let it bother us too much.

speaker: SS
words: What was Idaho like when you went to school?

speaker: GI
words: It was a lot smaller; I think we had between 1,500, 1,600 pupils.

speaker: GI
words: And in those days everybody said hello to everybody. They used to have

speaker: GI
words: these little that had" hello". Most friendly bunch of people you

speaker: GI
words: ever saw. Quite a change from high school, but it didn't take us

speaker: GI
words: long to blend in, I stayed at the old Hall, it's torn down now.

speaker: GI
words: It was a dormitory then and later on they made a co-op out of it.

speaker: GI
words: Wasn't a coop when I was going to school. Stayed there. The second

speaker: GI
words: year after I was there I got to working in the kitchen, hashing and

speaker: GI
words: scrubbing floors.

speaker: SS
words: Did you work your way through school?

speaker: GI
words: I was almost selfsupporting then after that. Well, see, my brother

speaker: GI
words: was just one year behind me and my sister one year behind him and

speaker: GI
words: then it ended up when I was a junior there was three of us up here.

speaker: GI
words: And my brother got a job working for a dairy and my sister got a job

speaker: GI
words: working for the veterinary at that time for the University working

speaker: GI
words: for room and board in the home. So, there wasn't a real big outlay

speaker: GI
words: but it was an effort. And we always had the support of our parents

speaker: GI
words: about going on to school. They never forced us, we just sort of

speaker: GI
words: grew up with that attitude; we wanted an education.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't get into a fraternity at all?

speaker: GI
words: No, I never joined a fraternity. My brother joined the TKEs later

speaker: GI
words: on. And neither sister joined a sorority either.

speaker: SS
words: That costs more money.

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, it costs more money.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a lot of why you didn't, or weren't you interested in it

speaker: SS
words: anyway?

speaker: GI
words: Well, I wasn't too much interested, really. I think one thing, the

speaker: GI
words: cost sort of bothered me and then I just didn't have the desire. Like

speaker: GI
words: some kids would come up here and if they didn't get to go to a frater

speaker: GI
words: nity they thought there was something bad about 'em. But I always

speaker: GI
words: remember one of our proctors, Bob Green, he's still living here around

speaker: GI
words: someplace- George Green. He got up in our dormitory dining room and

speaker: GI
words: he says, "it's rush week now. Some of you people aren't going to get

speaker: GI
words: a chance to go to a fraternity and some are. Some go to the fra

speaker: GI
words: ternity, it's going to go to their heads and they won't know you when

speaker: GI
words: you go to the fraternities. Remember if he's that kind of a person,

speaker: GI
words: he's not worth a tinker's damn." I thought about that; I knew some

speaker: GI
words: of the guys that pledged were just as friendly after they went to the

speaker: GI
words: fraternity and others it did go to their head. They thought that

speaker: GI
words: was something really out of this world.

speaker: SS
words: Did you feel that you were here just to learn? Or did you feel you

speaker: SS
words: were here to have a good time?

speaker: GI
words: I was coming here to learn, that was the idea of coming.

speaker: GI
words: We had some good times. You're bound to have good times. But basical

speaker: GI
words: ly you're supposed to learn.

speaker: SS
words: What were the kind of social good times that they had then?

speaker: GI
words: Well, we used to have, I don't know whether they still do or not.

speaker: GI
words: invite gals over to our dormitory to dinner and then dance afterwards.

speaker: GI
words: And then you'd be invited over to their place the next month or some

speaker: GI
words: thing. And then they had theirhall dances and they had dances down to

speaker: GI
words: the Student Union and the class dances. I learned to dance after I

speaker: GI
words: got up here, too. I don't know, seems like we went to lots of shows.

speaker: GI
words: And then I was quite active in the church group. The Wesley Foundation

speaker: GI
words: was the Methodist Church Group. In fact, that's where I met my wife.

speaker: GI
words: We had a party every Saturday night and social hour Sunday night and

speaker: GI
words: Sunday School in the morning. And it seemed like there was plenty of

speaker: GI
words: places to go and we seemed to have good times.

speaker: SS
words: Was she going to school here, too?

speaker: GI
words: Yes, she was a freshman the same time I was.

speaker: SS
words: From where?

speaker: GI
words: She was from the Boise Valley, down at Eagle, right close to Boise.

speaker: GI
words: Her name was Warren. At one time the Warren family had the most

speaker: GI
words: number of students from one family in the University till a guy by

speaker: GI
words: the name of Johnson came along and had one or two more. Brothers;

speaker: GI
words: her older brother was the first- nelCt to her older brother was the

speaker: GI
words: first president of Lindlay Hall when they first built it. Charley

speaker: GI
words: Warren. And then she had an older brother named George that graduated

speaker: GI
words: from law school. He's still living he's up in his eighties in Cali

speaker: GI
words: fornia.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a good deal of supervision of you young men and women when

speaker: SS
words: you were at the University then, or did you pretty much go their own

speaker: SS
words: way?

speaker: GI
words: The way they did; they controlled the girls, the girls had to be in

speaker: GI
words: at certain hours and that sort of controlled the boys. I guess they

speaker: GI
words: had to be in by midnight.

speaker: SS
words: I know that Dean French had quite a reputation.

speaker: GI
words: She was quite an old gal. I remember when I was a freshman and had

speaker: GI
words: this big deal on etiquette that she used to give every year and sometimes

speaker: GI
words: twice a year. The proctor saw that we were in to hear Dean

speaker: GI
words: French's lessons on etiquette; how to behave yourselves and all this

speaker: GI
words: kind of stuff.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get much out of it at the time?

speaker: GI
words: I guess we did. Of course, my mother was pretty strict on manners at

speaker: GI
words: the table and stuff like that, so it wasn't too shocking to me as it

speaker: GI
words: was to some, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it helped some?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes, it helped. Of course, we had a lot of fun about it, but it

speaker: GI
words: helps, I know it .It had to help.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard people say that they- that it was common for the boys to

speaker: SS
words: hate Dean French the whole time they were in school and then after

speaker: SS
words: they got out turn around and think she was the greatest person.

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, they gave her hell all the time but she was pretty good, really.

speaker: GI
words: She was really softhearted. I mean, if you'd go in her office and ask

speaker: GI
words: her about something she was real nice to you. But, boy, at these meetings

speaker: GI
words: she really laid it down.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that there was much fooling around, you know, like

speaker: SS
words: sleeping together before marriage, like there is these days?

speaker: GI
words: Not, too much. A Not too much.

speaker: SS
words: Not common. That's one thing that really seems to have changed.

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, it seems like they've sure changed a lot.

speaker: SS
words: Was the depression much of a pall over the way people were at that

speaker: SS
words: time? Did it seem to affect the students at the school? Because

speaker: SS
words: it was already started.

speaker: GI
words: I remember one kid that came to school and he played basketball, of

speaker: GI
words: course he got a scholarship, came here in an old Ford car and he

speaker: GI
words: didn't have money enough to run it and we'd throw our pennies together.

speaker: GI
words: Sometimes we'd buy a gallon of gas and we'd toot in that old car.

speaker: GI
words: One time I remember we had to push it downtown. There used to be a

speaker: GI
words: service station down at the foot of the hill down here where the electric

speaker: GI
words: company is down-there, down in there; we'd push it

speaker: GI
words: down the hill and we'd pool our money and get - gas was about 23c or

speaker: GI
words: 18 or 19c- get our pennies together and buy maybe two gallons of gas.

speaker: GI
words: We had a lot of fun. Everybody was the same. Everybody was hard up.

speaker: GI
words: I don t know, we had just as much fun then as when we had money.

speaker: SS
words: What were you studying then?

speaker: GI
words: I was taking business. Took business and ended up farming.

speaker: SS
words: So you weren't planning at the time to farm?

speaker: GI
words: No, I was going to go in the bank, was what I really intended to do.

speaker: GI
words: After I went home Dad wanted me to stay and help him farm and so I

speaker: GI
words: was going to help farm until my brother, came over to take over the

speaker: GI
words: farm and he came there a year or two and then he decided to go back

speaker: GI
words: to school and get his masters degree, and I don't know, I just naturally

speaker: GI
words: drifted into it. The war came in the early '40's, Dad said, "Well,

speaker: GI
words: I'll just run the farm over to you if you want to farm." So that's

speaker: GI
words: how I got to farming. I never intended to farm, but I haven t regretted

speaker: GI
words: it though. It's quite a business. Business education sure didn't

speaker: GI
words: hurt me in the farming. And I always worked real close with the

speaker: GI
words: county extension agent, program and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: So your brother didn't want to go back to farming then?

speaker: GI
words: No, not really. When he graduated he went to work tor

speaker: GI
words: the soil conservation service, then he got drafted in the army. Then

speaker: GI
words: when he came back he went to work for Libby's and he was a field man

speaker: GI
words: and buyer down on the coast for over twenty years for them. Then

speaker: GI
words: they retired him he dabbled in real estate and he's working for North

speaker: GI
words: west Packing Company. He quit it a while and just seemed to like to

speaker: GI
words: get back in it. He's quite a social guy and he knows his stuff in

speaker: GI
words: that line, buying for packers, fruit and vegetables and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have it in mind then to stay here if you possibly could?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, I intended to go in the bank at home when Dad was on the board,

speaker: GI
words: really. But I had a chance to go to work there once and my uncle

speaker: GI
words: sort of- The banker was going to have to be out of town and there was

speaker: GI
words: only one man there, one man was gone, and they had to have somebody

speaker: GI
words: to be in the bank by himself and there was another fellow who worked

speaker: GI
words: in a bank for years that was unemployed, so this guy the
