speaker: GI
words: experience, they better get him in the bank and then he got the second

speaker: GI
words: job after the this guy came back. He had to be gone a while. I just

speaker: GI
words: about got started there then. Then I got married; '35 it was.

speaker: SS
words: Things were pretty severe, I imagine, for your folks to decide you

speaker: SS
words: should really come home; or did you decide you should leave school?

speaker: GI
words: Well, yes, I sort of decided. My grades weren't too good and I wor-

speaker: GI
words: ried quite a lot about things my grades weren t very good that

speaker: GI
words: semester, and Dad just had to have some help.

speaker: SS
words: You were worried about the farm?

speaker: GI

words: a few years later down there. It took a while for them to get over

speaker: GI
words: but they still got their schoolhouse and the first six grades

speaker: GI
words: are down there yet. So it worked out real good, I think.

speaker: SS
words: You know talking about back in history, tradition, it seems like that

speaker: SS
words: the two towns always have had a bitter rivalry between them.

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, it all happened years ago. You've probably heard this story

speaker: GI
words: before; you see, Juliaetta was the first established town and they

speaker: GI
words: had the first post office down there. " they built the railroad

speaker: GI
words: down there they tried to get a right-of-way down there for a turntable

speaker: GI
words: to turn the train around because they couldn't make the ra ilroad

speaker: GI
words: clear to Lewiston then, and they was holding the railroad up and they

speaker: GI
words: couldn't get property and old guy name of Kirby owned the land where

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick was he says," I'll give you the (Land)." So the train for afew

speaker: GI
words: years turned around there at Kendrick even though when it went to

speaker: GI
words: Juliaetta it'd back up there and turn around and go back to Spokane.

speaker: GI
words: And that's really what gave the boost that started it. Kendrick got

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick

speaker: GI
words: a good start. I think probably there was some there be

speaker: GI
words: fore that, but that was the thing that gave it the boost.

speaker: SS
words: Then, it's really because Juliaetta people or something would hold

speaker: SS
words: the railroad up.

speaker: GI
words: That's the story I always heard, that that was what gave Kendrick the

speaker: GI
words: boost, because that was the end of the railroad.

speaker: SDS
words: I just wondered; it seemed to me that maybe part of the rivalry came

speaker: SDS
words: from the fact that the towns are so close. They're both pretty good

speaker: SDS
words: size.

speaker: GI
words: They always had a fight; the old baseball teams- oh, Juliaetta and

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick baseball, each town used to have a town team, you know, baseball.

speaker: GI
words: And I remember them telling about it. And I saw some of it

speaker: GI
words: after I was old enough, after I got in school. They always had their

speaker: GI
words: fights over the old town team baseball.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a regular Sunday—?

speaker: GI
words: Yeah, that was a Sunday afternoon deal. And there was quite a lot of

speaker: GI
words: rivalry there. And then in the wintertime you used to couldn't get

speaker: GI
words: frcm Kendrick to Juliaetta on the road, it would get wet and it was

speaker: GI
words: muddy and so forth and that was when I was in high school when that

speaker: GI
words: road was first graveled, that they had a graveled road between Kendrick

speaker: GI
words: and Juliaetta. And then sometimes when I was in high school you

speaker: GI
words: hardly couldn t get through there^it would be so darn muddy. It's hard to

speaker: GI
words: believe now.

speaker: SS
words: Did the towns, do you feel like, that really between the people in the

speaker: SS
words: towns there was really a strong rivalry or was there a lot of friend

speaker: SS
words: ship underneath that? Or was it really more rivalry?

speaker: GI
words: More rivalry, I think. There was some of 'em that were real bitter;

speaker: GI
words: but there was rivalry. Now you take the t^epjione company in Kendrick

speaker: GI
words: was bought by the Schupfers, and they lived in Juliaetta, and things

speaker: GI
words: like that. They worked together but there was a lot of rivalry. Like

speaker: GI
words: events and things, especially the baseball and other things. I think

speaker: GI
words: some of the old-timers all thought Juliaetta should have been the

speaker: GI
words: bigger town. Of course, Juliaetta now is gaining population, but the

speaker: GI
words: business center is more in Kendrick. And Kendrick always had the doc

speaker: GI
words: tor and Kendrick had the dentist and the banks. Of course, they used

speaker: GI
words: to have a bank down there too. There's more business there; more

speaker: GI
words: stores.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have an idea why that's developed that way? That Kendrick has

speaker: SS
words: become the business center, and Juliaetta has kind of ?

speaker: GI
words: I don't know, I just don't know, I've often wondered about that.

speaker: GI
words: Listening to what my dad said and so forth, it probably started that

speaker: GI
words: way at the time of the railroad thing. Because it always did have

speaker: GI
words: the biggest business center. Probably the people had something to do

speaker: GI
words: with it. Maybe a little different attitude, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: When you were going to school in Kendrick did you take part in any

speaker: SS
words: town activities outside of school? In high school?

speaker: GI
words: I can't remember if I did, much.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't dance and that kind of thing much?

speaker: GI
words: I went to church; got to going to church there some and stuff like

speaker: GI
words: that. Of course, at that time they had two theatres in Kendrick

speaker: GI
words: and we'd go to the shows and stuff like that. As far as taking part

speaker: GI
words: in any activities of the town, I never got started taking part

speaker: GI
words: in of the activities of the town. After I was back on the farm

speaker: GI
words: and started- well, after I got on this school reorganization committee

speaker: GI
words: then I started going to the Chamber of Commerce and I started briefing

speaker: GI
words: them on what the progress and what was happening to the school reorganization.

speaker: GI
words: So I gave a presentation when had the plan of what

speaker: GI
words: we were going to do and one of the guys got up and said, "We make a

speaker: GI
words: motion that we give him a three year membership in the Chamber of Com

speaker: GI
words: merce for what he's done for Kendrick." And that's how I got started

speaker: GI
words: there. Then when we had the reorganization .to form the board of direch

speaker: GI
words: tors- all these schools, I think there was actually- well, there must

speaker: GI
words: have twenty districts involved or more, more than that involved. There

speaker: GI
words: was part of Nez Perce County, the one district out of Clearwater County

speaker: GI
words: and then all of Latah County. I believe we had parts of twentyfour

speaker: GI
words: districts- of the old districts, and so each district had to have

speaker: GI
words: a trustee zone and there was a trustee for each district. And so

speaker: GI
words: they met together, the whole community meeting, and decide who was

speaker: GI
words: going to be on the board of directors. One guy got up and said,

speaker: GI
words: "Gerald Ingle thinks this thing'11 work, let's put him on that board

speaker: GI
words: of directors." I was already on one of the local school district

speaker: GI
words: boards that were coming in, so that's how I got on the Kendrick School

speaker: GI
words: District Board. So, we had a five member board. And then I got pret

speaker: GI
words: ty active in the affairs in Kendrick after that. I've been active

speaker: GI
words: ever since.

speaker: SS
words: Was it clear to everyone on the board that it made more sense to go

speaker: SS
words: and put the High School in Kendrick and the grade school in Juliaetta?

speaker: SS
words: Or the other way around?

speaker: GI
words: Well, it took a year gr two. We had a school at Leland. At the time

speaker: GI
words: I was in high school they had a two year high school at Leland; high

speaker: GI
words: school in Southwick and a high school in Kendrick. The Leland high

speaker: GI
words: school had folded up and Southwick's high school was coming into

speaker: GI
words: Kendrick on an agreement at the time of the consolidation. They quit

speaker: GI
words: and they were coming in on an agreement per pupil day. That just

speaker: GI
words: left the two; there was Kendrick and Juliaetta. But we had to keep

speaker: GI
words: the Leland grade school. We kept the Gold Hill school and the Crescent

speaker: GI
words: school,^rural schools. And there was another school we keptand

speaker: GI
words: the Southwick. Then we gradually, a year or two then, the people

speaker: GI
words: from Leland decided they wanted to come into Kendrick.

speaker: GI
words: And then it was the center and the bigger school.

speaker: GI
words: One of the last things I ever did was when they closed up Southwick,

speaker: GI
words: that was the last hearing I conducted when I was on the school board.

speaker: GI
words: I had already been voted a replacement and the new director hadn't

speaker: GI
words: taken his place when we had that hearing to do away with the grade

speaker: GI
words: school up there. You see, the state changed its financial deal;

speaker: GI
words: they gave so much per schoolroom unit; we had two rooms up there/^

speaker: GI
words: three grades and then the fourth, fifth and sixth in another. Well,

speaker: GI
words: the population had dwindled down so the state wouldn't pay us only

speaker: GI
words: for one schoolroom unit and we had to have two teachers, anyway, al

speaker: GI
words: most, the way it was worked out. So they decided- in fact, they were

speaker: GI
words: going to count that registration right in with the Kendrick registra

speaker: GI
words: tion so they wouldn't get credit for that unit up there at all. So

speaker: GI
words: there was no way for the board to go but to close that. I remember

speaker: GI
words: that hearing, I conducted that one, and, boy, I really got ironed out

speaker: GI
words: two or three times. It worked out pretty good. We had the two teachers

speaker: GI
words: teaching up there and took 'em into the system and it worked out real

speaker: GI
words: well. Iwas chairman of the board and the guy that took my place

speaker: GI
words: said, "I'm sure glad it was yuou that conducted that meeting, not me."

speaker: GI
words: He lived up there.

speaker: SS
words: That was Southwick?

speaker: SS
words: Southwick. That must have been '65. It went quite a while.

speaker: SS
words: Talking about getting ironed out; it strikes me that that happens an

speaker: SS
words: awful lot to a guy in public office. Ijust wonder if you have any

speaker: SS
words: thoughts about it now, representing what the law is.

speaker: SS
words: It didn't bother me as much as it does now, probably. I used to tell

speaker: SS
words: 'em that Iknew people were going to talk about somebody, and if they

speaker: SS
words: were going to talk, they just as well talk about me, and it's not

speaker: SS
words: going to hurt me as much as^he other person. But, I've gotten by pret

speaker: SS
words: ty well, but what gets me is when they start saying I'm dishonest or

speaker: SS
words: something like that; I've always tried to be honest and I've always

speaker: SS
words: tried to be fair with people. Do^as you would be done by. That has

speaker: SS
words: always been sort of my philosophy. This is my thirty-ninth year of

speaker: SS
words: being a public official, counting the schooljf and I think finally

speaker: SS
words: after a while it will finally get to you, best to take a rest from

speaker: SS
words: that.

speaker: SS
words: Thirty-ninth year? Does that mean starting thirty-nine years ago,

speaker: SS
words: or were there different—?

speaker: GI
words: Thirty-nine years counting- see, I was on the school board twentyeight

speaker: GI
words: years, and then a commissioner twenty years, they overlap.

speaker: GI
words: I said, elected official. I started in '37, that was when I went on

speaker: GI
words: the school board, I'm counting that as being an elected official.

speaker: SS
words: How did you decide to run as county commissioner?

speaker: GI
words: I took quite an active interest in county affairs and I'd been on the

speaker: GI
words: Fair Ba9rd fifteen years, and then on the County ASC Committee five

speaker: GI
words: years ve been quite active in the county, so I finally decided,

speaker: GI
words: well, why not run for county commissioner? And I'fife enjoyed it.

speaker: SS
words: Was it easy to get the party nomination at that time?

speaker: GI
words: No, boy, they had tried; there'd been petitions worked up for me to

speaker: GI
words: go to the legislature and for county commissioner before, but I

speaker: GI
words: would never sign it. And so, I went to this meeting down here and

speaker: GI
words: George Brocke, you've heard talk about George, Jr.'s father was in

speaker: GI
words: the legislature and I came to this Democrate meeting and I said,

speaker: GI
words: "You know, I've decided I'll run for county commissioner." And,

speaker: GI
words: boy, they just whooped and hollered. I had a pretty tough election

speaker: GI
words: that time, I only won by seventy-five votes, but the next time I won

speaker: GI
words: and the same guy ran against me, over a thousand votes and after that

speaker: GI
words: I never had any by either party, so got by pretty well.

speaker: SS
words: Did it affect your farming very much?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes. I could have made a lot more money by staying home, but I

speaker: GI
words: had hired help and I had a real good helper, my wife was really

speaker: GI
words: cooperative. She's contributed a lot to my success. She's got a

speaker: GI
words: good background. But I had to hire a lot of help, I was there to

speaker: GI
words: boss, though. I did a lot of work myself.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find it since the years have gone on since you started thatand

speaker: SS
words: which was the first year that you were commissioner?

speaker: GI
words: '56; or it was '57.

speaker: SS
words: Has it taken more and more of your time as the years went on?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes, a lot more time, just an awful lot more. Then, you know, it

speaker: GI
words: was pretty simple. You didn't have planning and zoning; you didn't

speaker: GI
words: have solid waste and you didn't have all these other programs- people

speaker: GI
words: programs involved in. We didn t have to spend too much time.

speaker: GI
words: We had two regular meetings and then we were probably on call two or

speaker: GI
words: three more times in the month and there were some night meetings you

speaker: GI
words: had to attend. But now you can just make it fulltime if you want to,

speaker: GI
words: there's something for you to do all the time if you'd do it.

speaker: SS
words: Has the administrative paperwork that's increased? How would you

speaker: SS
words: describe that?

speaker: GI
words: Just more things being added. More responsibilities. You see, we

speaker: GI
words: didn't used to have Civil Defense; we didn't used to have, as I say,

speaker: GI
words: Planning and Zoning. Then they^counties responsible for Solid

speaker: GI
words: Waste Programs. And there's other different programs- Federal pro

speaker: GI
words: grams to get mixed up in all the time to work with. Lots simpler

speaker: GI
words: than it is now.

speaker: SS
words: People told me tsjht in the '30's the commissioners were kind of a

speaker: SS
words: last resort quite often. You know, people that needed work and were

speaker: SS
words: really destitute during the Depression.

speaker: GI
words: I think the county was more involved in welfare then than we are now.

speaker: GI
words: Of course, when I first came on the board, the state didn't take care

speaker: GI
words: Aid to Dependent Children and stuff like that. That was our bailiwick.

speaker: GI
words: If they were under sixty-five, those kind of fellows were under us,

speaker: GI
words: so actually welfare work has actually decreased to what it was then.

speaker: GI
words: We had more responsibility, had to work with more people. Now they

speaker: GI
words: have the ADC and the juvenile deal, too and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: county and the state, I mean, there is a very widespread feeling, of

speaker: SS
words: course,that North Idaho gets the short end of the stick from the

speaker: SS
words: state as a whole. I'm wondering how you feel about that from your

speaker: SS
words: experiences.

speaker: GI
words: Well, I don't think so. But, we think sometimes. Northern Idaho

speaker: GI
words: that they haven't worked on the state roads like they have in South

speaker: GI
words: ern Idaho, but they can build roads so much cheaper down there. We're

speaker: GI
words: getting our share; we got the Whitebird grade and we're going to have

speaker: GI
words: the Lewiston grade

speaker: GI
words: We get our share, probably.

speaker: SS
words: What's it like to work with the state? Do you find in your experien

speaker: SS
words: ces that they understand the local problems well?

speaker: GI
words: Some do. I've always had pretty good luck actually working with the

speaker: GI
words: state officials. I think one thing now that Governor Andrus has done,

speaker: GI
words: he has this one guy, this Rollie Mooney that is appointive laison between

speaker: GI
words: his office and county and city government comes in every

speaker: GI
words: once in a while to visit with us. I think that helps. He was at our

speaker: GI
words: convention last week in Lewiston the whole time, he attends the city's

speaker: GI
words: convention, gets a feeling of the local governments and so forth.

speaker: GI
words: something I ve never had any trouble really

speaker: GI
words: with the state, and I've been quite active with the legislatures and

speaker: GI
words: I always go down there two, three, four times a year when the legis

speaker: GI
words: lature's meeting and a lot of the people in there I've known for years

speaker: GI
words: and work with them. In other capacities in the state, when I was on

speaker: GI
words: the school board those years and some of those people were on the

speaker: GI
words: school board, some of them did the same way I did, they started down

speaker: GI
words: here^low and some of em went to the legislative way and some of 'em

speaker: GI
words: went the commissioner way. Of course, now it's changing a little bit

speaker: GI
words: as I get older, some of them are dropping out but a few years ago I

speaker: GI
words: just knew an awful lot of people in that legislature that I had worked

speaker: GI
words: with on other programs, like the ASC Program and the school board.

speaker: SS
words: ASC?

speaker: GI
words: That's the Agricultural-

speaker: SS
words: - stabilization.

speaker: GI
words: Yeah. And the Victdi program. I've been past president of the State

speaker: GI
words: Association.

speaker: SS
words: You said that you were working with that kind of thing when you started

speaker: SS
words: in farming. Was that mostly with the state or the university?

speaker: GI
words: The university and the county extension agent upstairs.

speaker: SS
words: Did many farmers do that, or was that unusual?

speaker: GI
words: There was quite a few. There was quite a few there was some wouldn t

speaker: GI
words: have anything to do with them, but there's quite a few.

speaker: SS
words: What did they do for you mostly?

speaker: GI
words: They kept you up on the research and the new seeds. Like we used to

speaker: GI
words: raise lots of clover seed and the bugs came in and starting eating it

speaker: GI
words: and they'd help us with what kind of sprays to use and this kind of

speaker: GI
words: stuff and different methods of farming. The University does lots of

speaker: GI
words: research in establishing new varieties of grains and wheats, barley

speaker: GI
words: and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: Were you trying them out?

speaker: GI
words: I've had tests in my fields several different times years.

speaker: SS
words: Does this kind of information spread fairly fast among the farmers,

speaker: SS
words: do you think?

speaker: GI
words: oh I think so. Like when I had tests plots out on place, we'd

speaker: GI
words: have field days for the whole community to come in and explain dif

speaker: GI
words: ferent things. The thing of it is- thinking of an incidence: One

speaker: GI
words: year they had a helicopter came in and there was these people from

speaker: GI
words: the University and the extension service to look at this clover field

speaker: GI
words: of mine, trying to figure out these bugs that were eating up the clo

speaker: GI
words: ver blooms and cutting the yield. A neighbor across the fence, I went

speaker: GI
words: over and told him, I says, "Come over and talk to these guys." Said,

speaker: GI
words: "Naw, I don't have^use for those guys. Those white collar guys, I

speaker: GI
words: don't pay any attention." The next year they figured out a spray, but

speaker: GI
words: he was using the same spray I was the next year. He knew where to'get'

speaker: GI
words: the help but he wouldn't have anything to do with it, he wouldn't be

speaker: GI
words: classes as being out there with them.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why that is.

speaker: GI
words: Just-

speaker: SS
words: Independent.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. Farmers a class pretty independent. They don't like to

speaker: SS
words: be told.

speaker: SS
words: And maybe he thinks of them as not really working for a living.

speaker: SS
words: Yes. But it was sort of funny. He wouldn't have anything to do with

speaker: SS
words: them but still the next year he was looking for spray to spray his

speaker: SS
words: clover.

speaker: SS
words: Was that tendency there in the early days, too, of farmers to try to

speaker: SS
words: keep up on the most progressive ideas?

speaker: SS
words: Some of 'em did and some the old ways suited 'em. All people aren't

speaker: SS
words: progressive. All people aren't leaders, some have to be followers.

speaker: SS
words: But they'll swear they won't have anything to do with a certain thing

speaker: SS
words: but if it works out for me they 11 be doing the - in a year or

speaker: SS
words: two. That's the way it works.

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you, too, about what you think of people in generalthe

speaker: SS
words: interest or lack of interest that people have in running their

speaker: SS
words: affairs- political, in government and that kind of thing. They talk

speaker: SS
words: a lot about apathy these days, and I'm wondering if that's anything

speaker: SS
words: new or how that's changed in through the years.

speaker: GI
words: You mean, people taking an interest on what's going on?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: GI
words: There's some people never pay much attention anyway, there's always

speaker: GI
words: been some that would take an interest. But I think probably people

speaker: GI
words: now are a lot more interested on what's going on than they used to be

speaker: GI
words: as a whole. I've made this statement several times: I wish the people

speaker: GI
words: that were thinking we were doing alright, would come in and talk with

speaker: GI
words: us. The ones we get are the ones that are against what we're doing.

speaker: GI
words: That's the ones we see, the other people say, "Well, they're doing alright,

speaker: GI
words: why bother." And there's getting to be a little more

speaker: GI
words: I think than there used to be.

speaker: SS
words: As far as, like say, being informed and knowing what's being done, it

speaker: SS
words: seems to me that- that typically, I have the impression that most

speaker: SS
words: people tend to not know very much what's being done or what's going

speaker: SS
words: on. Do you think it's always been that way?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes. Like people that's running their public office, they just

speaker: GI
words: say, "He's alright." and whatever he says is okay, and there's some

speaker: GI
words: watch out of the corner of their eye all the time. That's part of it

speaker: GI
words: I guess. You never will get all the public informed. I've noticed

speaker: GI
words: in the past things we've done, we've publicized it in the paper and yit'H

speaker: GI
words: on the air and notified by mail, and when it happens they say they

speaker: GI
words: never ever heard of it. Never heard of it!

speaker: SS
words: That's the big difference; communication's really easy now. In the

speaker: SS
words: teens and '20's most people probably didn't hardly get the paper.

speaker: GI
words: All they did was get the weekly paper. That weekly paper, that local

speaker: GI
words: paper was their news, and then when they began to get radios, of

speaker: GI
words: course, it was a little different. They weren't very well informed

speaker: GI
words: then, but they still could criticize their officials.

speaker: SS
words: What were the topics of discussion you've heard them raise?

speaker: GI
words: I think the one that you hear, and I've heard all my life is taxes.

speaker: GI
words: Wasting money; taxes. It's a lot different looking in than it is

speaker: GI
words: looking out. I think that's the thing that you hear the most of.

speaker: GI
words: They get their tax bill, they don't think anything about it till they

speaker: GI
words: get their tax bill and then that sets 'em off.

speaker: SS
words: That's something that the grownups talked about when you were a kid?

speaker: GI
words: Oh, yes. Still do. And, of course, they used to gripe about our roads.

speaker: GI
words: But there wasn't much we could do about it, used to be all mud roads,

speaker: GI
words: you know. The same , mud roads all the way to Moscow. I

speaker: GI
words: can remember when Kendrick's streets used to get to be about that

speaker: GI
words: much loblally in the street in the wintertime. Drive down there with

speaker: GI
words: a buggy and the front axle nearly to the mud in the old buggy. Then

speaker: GI
words: they'd come to these board crosswalks across the street that they got

speaker: GI
words: to go over like that, it was a mess, I tell you! I can remember when

speaker: GI
words: they paved the streets in Kendrick there. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did they use to talk a lot, exchange a lot of information about farming

speaker: SS
words: methods and how to do thing, how things should be done? Or was that

speaker: SS
words: more of a private than public thing?

speaker: GI
words: I think probablyKthen a lot of these guys considered that was their

speaker: GI
words: private business. They might figure out some way they could get a

speaker: GI
words: better yield,,but they was going to keep that a secret from the nei

speaker: GI
words: ghbors. I think that was sort of the attitude of some of the old

speaker: GI
words: timers. If they had a better way of doing it he didn't publicize

speaker: GI
words: it too much, because he wanted to get that little advantage. That's

speaker: GI
words: just my feeling.

speaker: SS
words: The advantage, it wouldn't affect the price on his crop. They might

words: get ahead a little bit.

words: I don't know, that just seemed to be the attitude of the old-timers.

speaker: GI
words: dwindled down a little bit. But they were pretty strong there during

words: here and they had about 1,000 members. Now, I bet they haven't got

speaker: SS
words: ple wanting to socialize in the hardtimes?

speaker: GI
words: their regular meetings they generally had dancing and entertainment

words: which didn't really cost 'em very much. Somebody in the group played

words: was when we joined the Grange in Deary. Old guy get up therewith

speaker: GI
words: his fiddle. He never charged anything and he didn't want any money,

words: but just do it for fun. Well, they had their problems, their business

words: problems but cheap entertainment helps a lot I think and socializing.

speaker: GI
words: some kind of a Grange officer ever since.

words: to go up and nail a flag on that guy's house, and my dad was one of

words: bitter about those people that wouldn't do anything. There wasn't

words: Was this in Cameron?

speaker: GI
words: No, this

speaker: SS
words: This was right on the Ridge there?

words: Yes. And he was German. This guy was a German and he wouldn't buy

words: any Liberty Bonds and he talked for the Germans. And they made a

words: good boy out of him. Pretty drastic, I guess! But you know what

words: kind of a feeling it gave that guy when all these neighbors came in

words: yand put a flag on his house!

speaker: SS
words: tic then.

words: if you weren't patriotic there was something the matter with you.

words: Do you think even moreso than the Second World War, or was it about

speaker: SS
words: the same?

speaker: GI
words: youngetf . Seemed to me like that could be- at the First World

speaker: GI
words: War the feeling was stronger or maybe just because I was young that

speaker: GI
words: it made more impression on me.

speaker: GI
words: much land. Now there's two families on the old home place, I have

words: it rented to another family, but two families live on it where there

words: district and they have about, counting the high school and grade

words: school, I don't suppose they have over a dozen.

words: ners. Of course, they have their local telephone company, you know,

words: called that. I remember them talking about it. I remember Dad and

words: the hired man went to one meeting; they had an IWW meeting up in the

words: their bedrolls, you know and they'd sleep in the barn, sleep - or roll

words: em out in the open or  the haystack. They didn't stay but three

words: Well, first we had our chores to do, but not till I got a little older

words: then I got so that I had to work; thirteen, fourteen, and in there.

words: for us, a lot of the people working there were people from the northern

words: down on the Ridge with this threshing crew, work down there ear

words: lier then some of 'em have to quit and go back and harvest their crop.

words: But a lot of 'em were people from that area.

words: really asked anybody-but it seems like there were all these different

words: things going on at once, probably had to work pretty closely together

words: to make sure everything went well.

words: work. When they g$t done, certain guys rolled up the belt, one turned

words: the blower down. One did this and one did this and one hooked onto

words: the engine on the machine; everybody had their job, you know. They'd

words: crew you wanted because the guys that moved like that would

words: wegians there and then say north of us there were Swedes. I was amu

words: of my grandfather.

words: Deary there a little ways; the old Wells place, I remember. Then

words: knew Chuck real wweellll. AAnr^d It usedj to pasture cattle back there and

words: Yeah.

words: otherwise from others?

words: was.

words: he did have a little too much.

words: outspoken, and they didn't push much over on him.

words: They wanted a line to go in there, take part of that district. So

words: trict. Said, "Your kids are going to Lapwai, they don't want to comedon't

words: move this line up closer to Leland." And I got a hold of , I

words: to take more." So we got— (Chuckles)

words: surprised me.

speaker: GI
words: She looked after

words: She said that there a stuff going on. She had a
